definition: A person or business that might become a customer. A lead can come from a form, funnel, call, ad, referral, website chat, social media message, import, or manual entry.
example: A homeowner fills out a seller valuation form. That person is now a real estate lead.

definition: The person record inside the CRM. It usually stores name, phone, email, tags, notes, tasks, custom fields, conversations, and appointment history.
example: Maria Santos is a contact with the tag Dental Implant Interest.

definition: Customer Relationship Management. In plain English, it is the organized database where a business tracks leads, customers, conversations, tasks, and sales activity.
example: A clinic uses the CRM to see who requested a consultation and who still needs follow-up.

definition: A label added to a contact for organization, segmentation, or automation. Tags should have a clear purpose.
example: New Lead, No Show, VIP Customer, and Whitening Interest are tags.

definition: A field created to store specific information that the business needs. Use fields for values, not broad labels.
example: Pet Type, Preferred Service, Budget, Property Address, and Insurance Provider are custom fields.

definition: A saved filtered view of contacts. It helps teams quickly find a useful group of people.
example: A smart list can show all new leads from the last seven days who have not booked.

definition: A set of fields that collects information from a lead. Keep forms short unless the business truly needs more details.
example: A grooming form asks for name, phone, pet type, service, and preferred appointment day.

definition: One question or input inside a form or contact record.
example: Phone Number is one field. Service Interest is another field.

definition: A focused path that guides a visitor toward one clear action, usually from landing page to form to thank-you page.
example: A dental implant funnel explains the offer, collects lead details, and invites the lead to book.

definition: The page where a visitor arrives after clicking an ad, post, email, or link. It should focus on one offer and one action.
example: A free buyer guide landing page asks visitors to enter their email.

definition: The page shown after a form is submitted. It confirms what happened and tells the lead the next step.
example: After submitting a clinic form, the thank-you page says to book a consultation.

definition: A phone inquiry from a possible customer. Calls should be tracked because missed calls can still become leads.
example: A missed call after business hours can trigger an automatic text-back.

definition: A paid promotion on platforms like Facebook, Google, Instagram, or TikTok that tries to attract attention and generate leads.
example: A dental clinic runs a Facebook ad for free implant consultations.

definition: A lead recommended by an existing customer, partner, friend, or another business.
example: A current grooming customer recommends a neighbor with a new dog.

definition: A visual tracker that shows where each deal or opportunity is in the sales process.
example: New Lead, Contacted, Booked, Won, and Lost are pipeline stages.

definition: The deal record connected to a contact. It tracks where the lead is in a pipeline.
example: Maria is the contact. Her implant consultation deal is the opportunity.

definition: The booking system that controls availability, appointment length, buffers, team assignment, and booking links.
example: A grooming shop calendar allows 60-minute appointments with a 15-minute buffer.

definition: A rule-based automation that starts from a trigger and runs actions.
example: When a form is submitted, send an SMS, create an opportunity, and notify staff.

definition: The event that starts a workflow.
example: Form Submitted, Tag Added, Appointment Booked, Customer Replied, and Missed Call are triggers.

definition: Something a workflow does after it starts.
example: Send SMS, Add Tag, Create Task, Move Opportunity, and Notify User are actions.

definition: A delay before the next workflow action.
example: Wait one day, then check if the lead booked an appointment.

definition: A rule that checks whether something is true before deciding what happens next.
example: If the contact has an appointment, stop the nurture messages.

definition: A URL that receives event data from another app. It is often used to send GHL data to Make, n8n, Zapier, or custom apps.
example: A GHL form submission sends lead details to a Make webhook.

definition: A structured way for software systems to talk to each other. Beginners should understand the concept before trying to code with it.
example: An API can let one tool create or update records in another tool.

definition: A visual automation platform useful for multi-step scenarios, data formatting, routers, and app connections.
example: Make can receive a GHL webhook, add a row to Google Sheets, and send a Slack message.

definition: A flexible automation platform often used by technical builders, including self-hosted workflows and custom logic.
example: n8n can receive a webhook, enrich lead data, and update another system.

definition: A beginner-friendly automation platform for connecting apps with simple triggers and actions.
example: Zapier can send a new GHL lead to a spreadsheet or email tool.

definition: A reusable package of GHL assets, such as workflows, funnels, fields, calendars, and pipelines, that can help set up new client accounts faster.
example: An agency can create a dental clinic snapshot and adapt it for each new clinic client.

definition: A separate client workspace inside an agency HighLevel account.
example: A marketing agency may have one sub-account for each local business client.
